info.topictitle: A1.2.7 Differences between DNA and RNA

markdown:
## Key Differences between DNA and RNA


1. Although both DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid) are nucleic acids built from nucleotide monomers, they differ in several key features.
2. These differences are both **structural**, and **molecular**. 

![DNA vs. RNA](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/55f0812ac5a48dd54b2d7bba543524d5bf6fe981-4608x3000.png)

## DNA vs. RNA At A Glance


| Feature | DNA | RNA |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Full Name | Deoxyribonucleic Acid | Ribonucleic Acid |
| Strands | Double-stranded | Single-stranded |
| Sugar | Deoxyribose (lacks one oxygen atom) | Ribose |
| Nitrogenous Bases | Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Cytosine (C), Guanine (G) | Adenine (A), Uracil (U), Cytosine (C), Guanine (G) |
| Base Pairing | A pairs with T; C pairs with G | A pairs with U; C pairs with G |
| Function | Stores genetic information for long-term use | Transmits genetic information for protein synthesis |
| Stability | Stable due to double-stranded structure | Less stable, designed for short-term use |
| Location | Mainly in the nucleus (some in mitochondria) | Found in the nucleus and cytoplasm |

<callout type="analogy">* Think of **ribose** as a high-performance sports car, fast and reactive, but less durable. 
* **Deoxyribose**, on the other hand, is like a sturdy SUV, built for stability and endurance.</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">* When asked to compare DNA and RNA, always organize your answer systematically into **strands, bases, sugars**. 
* IB markschemes award separate points for each.</callout>

<callout type="tok">How does DNA’s structural stability contribute to its role as a hereditary molecule? Could RNA ever replace DNA in this role? Why or why not?</callout>

<callout type="self_review">* How many strands are present in DNA and RNA?
* What are the four nitrogenous bases in RNA?
* What structural feature distinguishes ribose from deoxyribose?</callout>
